#  Relaxor ferroelectrics

Relaxor ferroelectrics (RFEs) have been extensively studied for capacitive energy storage due to their characteristic slim polarization-electric field (P-E) loops, which arise from polar nanoregions (PNRs) that minimize hysteresis loss while maintaining moderate polarization. However, a fundamental trade-off persists: the very nanoscale disorder that suppresses hysteresis also limits the achievable recoverable energy density (W_rec) because the maximum polarization is constrained by the random-field-driven breakdown of long-range ferroelectric order. Recent strategies, such as constructing multi-oriented nanodomains or embedding secondary phases, have pushed W_rec values in RFEs to competitive levels, yet these approaches often sacrifice efficiency (η) or require complex processing. The present work addresses this bottleneck by introducing heterogeneous dipolar structures (HDSs) within an antiferroelectric (AFE) matrix, effectively merging the low-hysteresis character of RFEs with the high-polarization capability of field-induced FE states. This design circumvents the intrinsic polarization ceiling of conventional RFEs while retaining their hallmark slim-loop behavior, offering a distinct pathway to simultaneously enhance W_rec and η.
